coredata.dc:title: Magnetic modulation on chiroptical activities of nematically assembled carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

Zhang, Huaifang
Cui, Yanyan
Wang, Weichao
Liu, Peizhao
He, Tingchao
Fang, Fan
Hao, Junjie
Chen, Wei
Li, Yiwen
Cheng, Jiaji
coredata.dc:description: Effectively harnessing the assembly of achiral carbon dots into a chiral manner is a prominent step for applying carbon dots into the area of stereoselective optoelectronics and theranostics. Herein, magnetic-modulated and circularly polarized luminescence (CPL)-active photonic thin films were presented in this article via co-assembly and magnetic-mediation strategy of cellulose nanocrystals, carbon dots and magnetic nanoparticles. The photonic bandgap of the composite films is modulated via interfacial interactions between the building blocks, and more efficiently via external magnetic field which can further enhance the selective reflection of the films with a maximum CPL anisotropic factor as high as −0.92, indicating the optimized condition for achieving CPL signals is basically when the photonic bandgap (PBG) are close to the emission peaks of nanocomposite films, which may essentially facilitate the selective reflection effect and leads to the output of opposite CPL signals. Such strategy would inevitably boost the development of carbon dots based chiral devices and reagents into the realm of chirality-related biological issues and next generation chiral optoelectronics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cellulose nanocrystals
Circularly polarized luminescence
Magnetic modulation
Selective reflection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Fe3O4/CNCs nanocomposites and Fe3O4 nanoparticles
Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots and magnetically modified CNCs nanorods
Construction of magnetic-modulated and CPL-active chiral photonic films
Magnetic modulation of chiral photonic films

Pingshan District Innovation Platform Project of Shenzhen Hi-tech Zone Development Special Plan in 2022 (29853M-KCJ-2023-002-01), and Natural Science Foundation of Top Talent of SZTU (grant no. GDRC202344). L. Xu, H. Zhang and Y. Cui contributed equally to this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) G-CDots, (b) Y-CDots and (c) R-CDots, respectively. Insets (bottom) are the corresponding high resolution TEM and Photographs of these water-soluble G-CDots, Y-CDots, and R-CDots in daylight (left) and under UV irradiation (right), respectively. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra. (e) Steady-state PL spectra. (f) Raman shift. (g) FTIR spectra. (h) XRD pattern of CNCs and the Fe3O4/CNCs containing the peaks of CNCs and Fe3O4 magnetic particles. (i) Magnetization curve of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CNCs NPs.

(a) Fluorescence microscopy images under 360 nm irradiation, and illustration is the photographs of photonic films taken under natural light and UV irradiation on a black background. (b) Reflective POM images of the CNCs-R-CDots composite films. (c–f) Transmission spectra, CD spectra (inserted is the normalized CD spectra), CPL spectra and g lum curves of CNCs photonic films containing different doping concentrations of R-CDots.

(a) Illustration of the tunable CD of films that is regulated by the pitch of films. The left-handed helical structure of photonic films shows positive CD signals (left) and schematic mechanism of the induced CPL which is the result of combined modulation of PL and PBG (right). (b–e) Transmission spectra, CD spectra (inserted is the normalized CD spectra), CPL spectra, and g lum curves of CNCs-R-CDots photonic films synthesized under different external magnetic field strength. (f–i) corresponding POM images of the CNCs-R-CDots composite films synthesized under different magnetic field. The POMs show typical fingerprint patterns of a cholesteric phase in all samples as highlighted by the red circle. (j–k) corresponding SEM cross-section images of the films (i) for CNCs-R-CDots-C3-0mT, (j) for CNCs-R-CDots-C3-7mT, (k) for CNCs-R-CDots-C3-12mT, and (l) for CNCs-R-CDots-C3-15mT. The pitch decreased from 552 nm to 398 nm with increasing magnetic field. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

A schematic illustration on synthesis of magnetic-modulated and CPL-active photonic thin films with multicolor. Through magnetic modulation and selective reflection, the CPL signal can be manipulated.

head.ce:title: Magnetic modulation on chiroptical activities of nematically assembled carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Engineering Physics, Shenzhen Technology University, Shenzhen 518118, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Integrated Circuits and Optoelectronic Chips, Shenzhen Technology University, Shenzhen 518118, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Integrated Circuits and Optoelectronic Chips, Shenzhen Technology University, Shenzhen 518118, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effectively harnessing the assembly of achiral carbon dots into a chiral manner is a prominent step for applying carbon dots into the area of stereoselective optoelectronics and theranostics. Herein, magnetic-modulated and circularly polarized luminescence (CPL)-active photonic thin films were presented in this article via co-assembly and magnetic-mediation strategy of cellulose nanocrystals, carbon dots and magnetic nanoparticles. The photonic bandgap of the composite films is modulated via interfacial interactions between the building blocks, and more efficiently via external magnetic field which can further enhance the selective reflection of the films with a maximum CPL anisotropic factor as high as −0.92, indicating the optimized condition for achieving CPL signals is basically when the photonic bandgap (PBG) are close to the emission peaks of nanocomposite films, which may essentially facilitate the selective reflection effect and leads to the output of opposite CPL signals. Such strategy would inevitably boost the development of carbon dots based chiral devices and reagents into the realm of chirality-related biological issues and next generation chiral optoelectronics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chiral carbon dots (CDots), as an emerging enatioselective nano-luminophore, has been rigorously studied in recent years [1–4]. Apart from intrinsically chiral CDots which shows generally weak chiral signals and suffers from undefined structures for understanding the chiral origins, the rest are obtained via either [5,6]: 1) ligand-induced chirality endowed by chiral organic ligands such as amino acids [7–10], or 2) assemblies of achiral CDots into chiral configurations by using chiral templates [11–14]. To date, Kang and colleagues synthesized chiral CDots via electrooxidation polymerization from serine enantiomers, which exhibited enantioselective catalytic activity on 3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine enantiomers oxidation [15]. Further, Feng and coworkers reported that chiral helical supramolecular hydrogels with adjustable pitch and diameter could be fabricated via the co-assembly of negatively charged chiral hydrogel and positively charged achiral carbon dots. The obtained nanofibers with the smallest helical pitch and the largest diameter showed the highest luminescence dissymmetry g-factors (g lum) up to −3.8 × 10-3, which could be used as a chiral detector to distinguish between left and right circularly polarized light [16]. However, as mentioned above, ligand-induced chiral CDots also exhibit relatively weak chiroptical behaviors such as low circular dichroism (CD) intensity (g CD∼10-5-10-3) and inert circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) activities, due to the mismatch between chiral stereocenter and luminesce-center along with low photoluminance quantum yield (PLQY) caused by ligand induced defects during synthesis. As for chiral templating of achiral CDots, it is more promising to obtain strong chiroptical activities owing to their more anisotropic symmetry and preserved PLQY. Among them, nematic assemblies of CDots with long range chiral organizations based on substrates such as cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) and liquid crystals (LCs) are often favored for their virtue of enhanced CD and CPL signals, i.e. g lum close to 2, and scaled-up productions at nano-micro level [17]. Nonetheless, to finely control over the chiroptical responses of such nanocomposites is still challenging because manipulating photonic bandgap of CNCs or LCs during the chiral assembly relies pretty much on the chemical environment and compositions [11,18–20]. Simple and effective means for modulation of their CD and CPL signals is overwhelmingly required for the design of next-generation chiral CDots based optoelectronic devices and biological reagents [21,22].

Herein, magnetic-modulated and CPL-active photonic thin films were presented in this article via simple co-assembly strategy of CNCs, CDots and magnetic nanoparticles as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Typically, we prepared three emissive CDots (named G-CDots, Y-CDots and R-CDots) with green, yellow, and red emissions by oxidative polymerization of three phenylenediamine isomers [m-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, and p-phenylenediamine], respectively. Then CDots with various colors together with as-synthesized Fe3O4/CNCs nanocomposites were added to pure CNCs, and eventually the CPL-active photonic thin films with magnetic-modulated characteristics were obtained after evaporation induced self-assembly procedure. Through magnetic modulation on photonic bandgap, the obtained g lum can reach as high as ∼0.92 by selective reflection of the thin film. This strategy would greatly extend the scope for selective reflection mediated CPL device design, and provide a silver bullet for fine manipulation of chiral co-assemblies with enhanced chiroptical behaviors.

Iron (Ⅲ) chloride, Iron chloride hexahydrate, P-Phenylenediamine, m-Phenylenediamine and o-Phenylenediamine were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Iron (Ⅱ) sulfate heptahydrate, Ammonia solution and Ethanol absolute were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.,Ltd. All reactions are processed with aqueous solution based on high purity deionized (DI) water.

Three kinds of carbon dots (CDots) were prepared according to reported procedures. For green emitting CDots (G-CDots), yellow emitting CDots (Y-CDots), red emitting CDots (R-CDots), 10 mL of 16 mM mPD, oPD, pPD and 50 μL of 100 mM FeCl3 were added to 45 mL of deionized water, respectively. The mixture was placed at 90 °C stirring for 10 h. Then, the crude products were centrifugated at 12000 rpm for 15 min to remove large aggregates and the mCDots were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Fe3O4/CNCs nanocomposites and Fe3O4 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: CNCs (60 g, 1.21 wt%) were diluted with 120 g of DI water and ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, then they were preheated to 70 °C in a three-neck flask and kept for 30 min under stirring in a nitrogen atmosphere. 0.17 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 0.11 g of FeSO4·7H2O were dissolved in 5 mL of DI water, mixed solution was added under extensive stirring. A 400 μL amount of NH3·H2O was diluted to 20 mL with DI water and dropwise added to the CNCs suspension and reacted for 2 h. The resultant solution was cooled to room temperature. The products were washed using DI water and ethanol, respectively. Then centrifugation was carried out to remove the impurity. Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared with the same procedure without CNCs.

Three kinds of multiple-color-emissive CDots were used in this work and marked as B-CDots, Y-CDots,and R-CDots, respectively. A certain amount of CDots were added to a CNCs suspension (3 mL, 3 wt%, ultrasonic treatment for 10 min) and stirred 3 h to obtain a homogeneous suspension. Then the composite homogeneous suspension was poured into a polystyrene Petri dish with a diameter of 3.5 cm and dried under ambient conditions for 3 days to obtain the photonic films. Magnetic field was used to tune the photonic band gaps of the photonic films. A certain amount of Fe3O4/CNCs suspension and three kinds of CDots contents of 13.3 % in the photonic films was added to a CNCs suspension. The mixture underwent ultrasound treatment for 10 min with 50 % energy output and stirred three hours. Afterward, the mixture was poured into a polystyrene Petri dish with a diameter of 3.5 cm. The final film was obtained by placing the Petri dish between two magnets that were parallel to each other. The drying time was about 3 days.

Reflection spectra and transmission spectra of the photonic films were measured on a UV–vis-NIR spectrometer (Lambda 1050+), where the photonic films were perpendicular to the incident beam during the measurement. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a XPS Escalab Xi + X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific (China) Co. Ltd). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the photonic films were recorded on a GeminiSEM 300 microscope. (Carl Zeiss Microscopy Ltd., England). Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted on a TGA/DSC3+ instrument at 10 °C/min in air. (METTLER TOLEDO, Switzerland) FT-IR was performed on a Nicolet Is10 Fourier transform-infrared spectrophotometer (Thermo, China). CPL spectra of the photonic films were measured using a JASCO CPL-300 spectrophotometer. (JASCO, Japan). CD spectra were recorded using a JASCO J-1500 spectropolarimeter (JASCO, Japan) with a scan rate of 20 nm/min. Raman spectroscopy was measured on a Renishaw plc/in Via microscope system with 633 nm laser excitation. (Renishaw plc, England) X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were acquired with an Empyrean Diffractometer. (Panaco, Netherlands). The zeta potential of the CNCs and CNCs/Fe3O4 was determined on a Zeta sizer Nano ZS90. (Malvern, England). Magnetization curves of the CNCs and CNCs/Fe3O4 were obtained from a PPMS DynaCool physical property measurement system at room temperature. (Quantum Design, America). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the Fe3O4/CNCs, CNCs and three emissive CDots were obtained using a Talos F200X G2 microscope (Thermoscientific, America). Polarized optical microscopy (POM) images of the photonic films and composite suspension were obtained on an XPF-550C polarized light microscope (Caikang Optics, China) Fluorescence microscopy of the photonic films was performed on a Leica DM IL LED inverted fluorescence microscope under excitation at 360 nm. Fluorescence spectra of the CDots dispersion and photonic films were measured on a FLS1000 (Edinburgh Instruments, England) fluorescence spectrophotometer under excitation at 355 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots and magnetically modified CNCs nanorods
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images in Fig. 1 a–c demonstrate a monodisperse size distribution of CPDs with spherical morphology. Typically, the as-synthesized CPDs show 2.8 ± 0.5 nm in diameter for G-CDots, 4.2 ± 0.7 nm for Y-CDots, and 37.8 ± 6.7 nm for R-CDots. Due to the lower self-polymerization binding energy, the size of red emissive p-CDots is much larger than that of yellow and green emissive CDots as for the smaller the binding energy is, the easier the monomer self-polymerizes into large size particles [23]. The lattice spacing is characterized by high-resolution TEM as inserted in which 0.21 nm spacing refers to the graphene (100) planes [24]. In addition, these three CDots exhibit colorless, primrose yellow, and brown colors in daylight while green, yellow and red emissions under UV irradiation as illustrated by the inserted images indicating their stable down-conversion emission capability.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of three CDots display similar absorption behavior in the UV region of 200–300 nm (high-energy area), while exhibit distinct absorption bands at 340 nm, 418 nm and 480 nm in the low-energy regions as shown in Fig. 1d. Here, the absorption band in the high-energy region is mainly ascribed to the π–π* transition of CC structures. Since the π–π* transitions of these three kinds CDots are similar, the absorption band positions in the high-energy region are the same [25]. However, the absorption band in the low-energy region is ascribed to the n–π* transition of CN, CNC or CO structures. Different surface states of these three kinds CDots result in the different n–π* transition, therefore leads to distinctively different absorption band positions in the low-energy region, which is in accordance with the previous report [26]. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements in Fig. 1e show that G-CDots and Y-CDots exhibited single emission band with peak wavelengths at 470 and 570 nm, respectively. R-CDots exhibited two emission bands at 505 nm and 624 nm respectively. In order to clarify the chemical structure of CDots, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) analysis was utilized. The full spectra presented in Fig. S1 show three typical peaks: C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (400 eV), and O 1s (531 eV). These findings indicate that the samples consisted of the same elements. In the high-resolution spectra, the C 1s band can be deconvoluted into three peaks corresponding to sp2 carbons (CC, 284.8 eV), sp3 carbons (CO/CN, 286.1 eV), and carboxyl carbons (COOH, 288.9 eV). Two types of N are observed in Fig for N 1s spectrum, which can be assigned to graphite N bond with 400.5 eV and Amino N bond with 399.2 eV. The O 1s band contains two peaks at 532.0 and at 532.5 eV for CO and CO, respectively. Accordingly, compared to the other two CDots, Y-CDots have the highest graphite N (Table S1). In addition, the Raman spectra in Fig. 1f exhibit two evident peaks around 1371 cm−1 and 1567 cm−1, which correspond to disordered structures and graphitic structures of carbon materials. The intensity ratio ID/IG was found that Y-CDots (2.35 %) were much larger than G-CDots (0.71 %) and R-CDots (0.26 %), indicating that yellow emissive Y-CDots have the highest graphite N content. Fig. 1g is the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra which reveals the OH/NH vibration mode at around 3440 cm1, as well as lower energy stretching vibrations of CN at 1630 cm−1, CN at 1440 cm -1. These results confirmed the presence of abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-rich functional groups on the surface of pristine CDots. Besides, according to FTIR analysis of CN bond at 1440 cm−1 and CN bond at 1630 cm−1, Y-CDots also exhibit higher ratio compared to the other two CDots confirming the graphite N content follows Y-CDots > R-CDots > G-CDots. Therefore, we assert that the narrowed HOMO-LUMO band gap of yellow emissive Y-CDots is mainly caused by the graphite N doping, while for R-CDots, the red shift is mainly caused by the particle size increasing [27].

Subsequently, refer to existing reports [28], Fe3O4 NPs were then seed-growth onto the surface of CNCs nanorods via electrostatic interactions. Fig. 1h shows the X-ray diffraction patterns of CNCs, Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CNCs, respectively. The characteristic peaks of CNCs shown at 2θ = 14.8°,16.7°,22.4° and 34.4° correspond to the (110), (110), (002) and (040) planes, respectively, exhibiting the typical diffraction pattern of cellulose crystal structure, However, for the Fe3O4 NPs and Fe3O4/CNCs hybrids, the characteristic peaks were observed at 2θ = 30.1°, 35.5°, 43.1°, 53.4°, 57.0° and 62.9°corresponded to (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes, respectively, which is consistent with the standard pattern of Fe3O4 (JCPDS 89-0688) [29]. Compared to Fe3O4, Fe3O4/CNCs nanocomposites exhibited three different characteristic peaks (2θ = 14.8°, 16.7° and 22.4°）referring to different crystal planes, namely (101), (10−1), and (002), of CNCs nanorods. Further FTIR measurements (Fig. S2a) also confirms the chemical structure of CNCs, Fe3O4 NPs and Fe3O4/CNCs hybrids. Moreover, adhesion of the nanoparticles to the surface of CNCs can be confirmed directly from the TEM images in Fig. S3. The superparamagnetic property of Fe3O4/CNCs was proved by the hysteresis loop obtained from the vibrating sample magnetometer study. The thermal stability of CNCs, Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CNCs hybrids was studied under air atmosphere and the TGA curves are shown in Fig. S2b. The first degradation step with temperature below 150 °C is attributed to the evaporation of absorbed water, with weight loss of 1.22, 5.45 and 7.22 wt% for Fe3O4, CNCs and Fe3O4/CNCs hybrids, respectively. The second degradation step observed at higher temperatures in the case of CNCs and Fe3O4/CNCs hybrids is caused by the thermal decomposition of CNCs. The approximate weight content of Fe3O4 in the hybrids can be calculated using the following equation [30]:
(1)
f
hybrids
= x
f
F e 3 O 4
+
1 - x
f
CNCs
where fhybrids , fFe3O4 and fCNCs represent the percentage of the weight loss of Fe3O4/CNCs hybrids, Fe3O4 NPs, and CNCs at 600 °C, respectively. x is the weight content of Fe3O4 NPs in the hybrids. Based on Eq. (1), it was estimated that 13.38 wt% content of Fe3O4 NPs was incorporated on the surface of CNCs.

In addition, energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) measurements were performed on the Fe3O4 NPs and Fe3O4/CNCs hybrids, the result is shown in Fig. S4. For Fe3O4 NPs, the elemental of iron was detected to be 73.37 %, and a small amount of iron was detected in the Fe3O4/CNCs hybrids, this is because a small number of magnetic particles are adsorbed on the surface of CNCs nanorods, which is similar to the results from TGA measurement. In addition, the small number of magnetic particles changed the potential value of CNCs, compared to Fe3O4/CNCs, the Zeta potential of CNCs has a larger absolute value shown in Fig. S5d–e. The saturation magnetization (Ms) value for the Fe3O4/CNCs is 7.44 emu g−1, much less than that of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles (67.45 emu g−1), which is coincident with the small amount of Fe3O4 in the Fe3O4/CNCs shown in Fig. 1i [31]. This is mainly due to the presence of the CNCs, the non-magnetic CNCs weakens the interaction between magnetic particles, thereby reducing the saturation magnetization strength of Fe3O4/CNCs composite nanoparticles. However, the saturation magnetization strength of Fe3O4/CNCs can still achieve solid–liquid separation. As shown in Fig. S6, under an external magnetic field the pure CNCs solution do not exhibit the magnetic separation phenomenon, but the Fe3O4 NPs and Fe3O4/CNCs hybrids solution still could be magnetically separated by an external magnet and result in clear and transparent solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of magnetic-modulated and CPL-active chiral photonic films
ce:section.ce:para: A series of composite films were then prepared by drying mixtures of the CNCs suspensions and CDots solution with different concentration ratios at room temperature through evaporation induced self-assembly. The composite films were named CNCs-X-CDots −Y, where X represents a kind of CDots and took the values G, Y, and R. Y represents the ratio of CDots and took the values C1, C2, C3, C4 and C5 for CDots volume content of 6.25 %, 9.09 %, 11.76 %, 14.28 % and 16.67 % in the photonic films, respectively. To avoid redundancy, CNCs-R-CDots films were typically presented as shown in Fig. 2 which exhibit red color when excited by 360 nm UV light and structural color under natural light (see inserted images in Fig. 2a). Due to the electrostatic attractions, fluorescence microscopy images show a uniform fluorescence distribution indicated the CDots were well distributed in the CNCs-R-CDots films (See Fig. S7 for CNCs-G-CDots and CNCs-Y-CDots films). Further Zeta potential measurements of G-CDots, Y-CDots, R-CDots and CNCs (13.9, 22.3, 16.2 and −26.5 mV) demonstrate charge attractions between CDots and CNCs as shown in Fig. S5a–c [17]. Further, PL spectra shown in Fig. S8 showed that CNCs-G-CDots composite film exhibited single emission band with a peak wavelength at 518 nm while CNCs-Y-CDots and CNCs-R-CDots exhibited two emission bands, with the peak wavelengths at 546 nm and 473 nm for CNCs-Y-CDots, and at 593 nm and 475 nm for CNCs-R-CDots, respectively.

Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2b, the obvious fingerprint texture (highlighted by red circles) as well as strong birefringence can be observed from the POM image, which indicates the formation of a chiral nematic phase, and similar fingerprint structures can be observed as well in CNCs-G-CDots and CNCs-Y-CDots composite films as shown in Fig. S9 [32]. More interestingly, Fig. 2c indicated that with the increase of CDots doping concentration for the CNCs-R-CDots composite film, the intensity of the transmission peak increases, and the center of the transmission peak is blue-shifted. Similar phenomena can be also confirmed in their absorption spectra as shown in Fig. S10. This is because CDots and CNCs are oppositely charged, and as the concentration of CDots increases, Zeta potential increases, leading to a decrement in the electrostatic repulsion between the CNCs nanorod, which brings in a decrease in the pitch value. Since the wavelength of selective reflection follows Bragg equation, which can be expressed as:[33]
(2)
λ max
=
n
avg
P sin θ
where
n
avg
is the average refractive index of the hybrid film, P is the as-mentioned helical pitch, and θ is the angle between the surface of photonic film and incident light. Decrement of helical pitch would as a result lead to the blue-shifted of PBG [34,35].

For chiroptical property, CD measurements (Fig. 2d) are applied and show strong positive chiral responses in all photonic films, which corresponded to the left-handed chiral nematic structures. As the concentration of doped CDots increases, the CD signal is blue shifted from 595 nm to 383 nm for CNCs-G-CDots, 610 nm to 317 nm for CNCs-Y- CDots (Fig. S11) and 658 nm to 505 nm for CNCs-R- CDots, respectively. This tendency is basically in line with the transmission and absorption measurements shown in Fig. 2c and Fig. S10, which can be interpreted as: CD signal is corelated with absorption(transmission) behavior of the chiral films, therefore when PBG is changed by pitch variation, the absorption(transmission) of the films would change as well. When the concentration of doped CDots increases, the overall Zeta potential of the system will increase because CDots exhibit positive Zeta values (+13.9, +22.3 and +16.2 mV for green, yellow, and red CDots respectively, Fig. S5), leading to a decrement on electrostatic repulsion between CNCs as well as pitch length, which as a result brings in the blue-shift of CD signals as summarized in Fig. 3 a [36–39].

On the other hand, due to the selective reflection of CNCs films, CPL activities are observed as well (Fig. 2e) in which strong negative CPL peaks are recorded as −1620, −1180 and −3455 mdeg for CNCs-G-CDots-C3, CNCs-Y-CDots-C3 and CNCs-R-CDots-C3, respectively. To compare the CPL generation capability of these photonic films, luminescence dissymmetry factor of CPL is defined as g lum = 2 (IL − IR)/(IL + IR), where IL and IR are the emission intensity of LCP and RCP light, respectively [32]. The glum value can range from +2 for an ideal LCP light to −2 for an ideal RCP light, while g lum = 0 relates to inactive chiral luminescence. The results in Fig. 2f, and Fig. S12 show that the maximum g lum value is around −0.24, −0.17, and −0.37 for CNCs-G-CDots-C4, CNCs-Y-CDots-C4and CNCs-R-CDots-C3. This is because the nature of CNCs-induced selective reflection would give rise to the ability of forbidden propagation of l-CPL by selectively reflection and transmission of R-CPL that matches the chirality of their helical structures leading to the amplification of opposite glum value. Moreover, the principle of such effect is dominated by the microstructures of the CNCs film, i.e., the pitch value and PBG. Therefore, as indicated by Fig. 3a, when the PBG is far from the PL emission peak of nanocomposite films, the observed CPL signal as well as g lum would be small, whereas when they are overlapped it will greatly facilitate the selective reflection effect and leads to opposite CPL signals with enhanced g lum value [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic modulation of chiral photonic films
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the merit that one can modify PBG via pitch length to control the chiroptical behaviors of nanocomposite films, we then simply employ magnetic nanoparticles into the assembled films to manipulate the selective reflection by changing the pitch length as illustrated in Scheme 1. Typically, the film was prepared by adding 11.76 % CDots volume content and Fe3O4/CNCs into CNCs and evaporating and drying under external magnetic field of different strength, and these composite films were named as CNCs-X-CDots-Y-Z, where X and Y are the same as above, and Z represents the intensity of external magnetic field strength during film drying and noted as 0, 7, 12 and 15 for magnetic field strength of 0, 7, 12,15 mT. By increasing external magnetic field, the pitch length is decreased and the corresponding transmission (Fig. 3b), absorption (Fig. S13) and CD (Fig. 3c) peaks are blue-shifted from 612 to 501 nm in CNCs-R-CDots films. And similar phenomena are observed in CNCs-G-CDots and CNCs-Y-CDots composite films, for which the CD peaks blue-shifted from 610 to 480 nm and 560 to 400 nm, respectively. Subsequently, the CPL signal (Fig. 3d) of the films is enhanced from −2960 to −5475 mdeg along with g lum (Fig. 3e) reaches to approx. −0.92 which is almost 2.5 times enhancement compared to untreated samples (See Fig. S14 and Fig. S15 for CD and CPL measurements of CNCs-G-CDots-C3 and CNCs-Y-CDots-C3 films which could exhibit g lum close to −0.22 and −0.23 with nearly doubled increment compared to their untreated samples). Similarly, we assert such enhancement is attribute to the variation of pitch length, by which the selective reflection effect was improved when the PBG wavelength is in the vicinity of the emission wavelength of CDots as discussed before in Fig. 3a [40].

To deep it further, POM images in Fig. 3f–i, Fig. S16 and Fig. S17 show that under different magnetic field, as the strength of the external magnetic field increases, the suspensions of R-CDots, CNCs, Fe3O4/CNCs suspensions showed an enhanced response to the applied magnetic field. Therefore, when the suspension is dried into a film, these samples showed more consistent color uniformity from multicolor to a brownness color with increasing magnetic field and the finger texture is denser, confirming the regulation of helical pitch value [40]. The structure of the films was then characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) on the cross-section of the films. The SEM images showed characteristic Bouligand curves of chiral nematic self-assembly, thereby confirming the helical architecture of the films are preserved. The pitch was measured as the distance between the layers that the nanorods rotated for 360° in the SEM images. The pitches were about 552 nm for CNCs-R-CDots-C3-0, 475 nm for CNCs-R-CDots-C3-7, 455 nm for CNCs-R-CDots-C3-12, and 398 nm for CNCs-R-CDots-C3-15, as measured in Fig. 3j–m, respectively. Besides, the pitch of films cross-section is counted, ten places were chosen randomly over the SEM cross-section image, and pitch sizes were measured three times at each position in Fig. S18. The decline in pitch with an increase in the external magnetic field strength was consistent with previous CD and CPL results, showing that the magnetic-mediation strategy can effectively control over the morphology and PBG of the composite films without disturbing the co-assembly of nematic structures. Therefore, one can effectively manipulate both CD and CPL signals of these composite films with greatly improved anisotropic factors induced by high quality film processing and tunable pitch length.

In conclusion, magnetic-modulated and CPL-active photonic thin films with tunable and enhanced chiroptical properties were synthesized via co-assembly and magnetic-mediation strategy. Further investigation indicated: 1) By tuning concentrations of CDots and external magnetic field, the pitch value and PBG of the composite films are modulated via interfacial interactions between the building blocks exhibiting CD and CPL signals in a controlled manner; 2) The observed CPL behaviors can be further enhanced with a g lum,max factor as high as −0.92 when the magnetic modulation makes the PBG match with the emission wavelength of photonic films. These findings not only provided an effective and efficient approach for design of CPL-active photonic films with controlled/enhanced chiroptical responses but also prominently triggered the development of chiral quantum dots based applications such as chirality-related biological therapy and 3D display devices in the near future.

Lihai Xu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Huaifang Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Yanyan Cui: Investigation, Data curation. Weichao Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Peizhao Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Tingchao He: Resources, Methodology. Fan Fang: Software, Data curation. Junjie Hao: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Wei Chen: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology. Yiwen Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jiaji Cheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62204107), National Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (No. 2020CFB200, No. 2021CFB018 and 2023AFB635). Pingshan District Innovation Platform Project of Shenzhen Hi-tech Zone Development Special Plan in 2022 (29853M-KCJ-2023-002-01), and Natural Science Foundation of Top Talent of SZTU (grant no. GDRC202344). L. Xu, H. Zhang and Y. Cui contributed equally to this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://www.journals.elsevier.com/journal-of-colloid-and-interface-science/. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.09.144.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colloidal chiral carbon dots: an emerging system for chiroptical applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhao; J. Xie; Y. Tian; S. Mourdikoudis; N. Fiuza-Maneiro; Y. Du; L. Polavarapu; G. Zheng, Colloidal Chiral Carbon Dots: An Emerging System for Chiroptical Applications. Adv. Sci., 11 (13), (2024) 2305797.

N. H. Cho; A. Guerrero-Martínez; J. Ma; S. Bals; N. A. Kotov; L. M. Liz-Marzán; K. T. Nam, Bioinspired chiral inorganic nanomaterials. Nat. Rev. Bioeng., 1 (2), (2023) 88-106.
